original_hypothesis.title: No Minimum Owner Count Allows Permanent Wallet Bricking via Owner Removal
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: removeOwnerAtIndex has no check to ensure at least one owner remains. A single owner (or the last remaining owner) can remove ALL owners including themselves. With zero owners, no one can call any onlyOwner function (addOwner, execute, upgrade), permanently locking all funds with no recovery mechanism.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: The removeOwnerAtIndex function only checks that an owner exists at the given index (NoOwnerAtIndex), deletes it, and emits an event. There is no counter tracking active owners and no minimum-count enforcement. After all owners are removed, _checkOwner always reverts (isOwnerAddress returns false for any address, and msg.sender != address(this) for external calls).

proof_of_signal.pattern: removeOwnerAtIndex deletes owner without checking remaining owner count; no activeOwnerCount state variable exists

preconditions: Caller is a current owner of the wallet
Caller knows the indices of all current owners

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Owner removal without minimum count enforcement
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: delete _getMultiOwnableStorage().isOwner[owner] and delete ownerAtIndex[index] with no minimum-1 check
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No active owner counter, no require(remainingOwners >= 1) check

analysis_questions: Can the last owner remove themselves via a single transaction?
Is there any other code path that could re-add an owner after all are removed?
Could the EntryPoint path somehow bypass the owner check to add a new owner?
validation_requirements: Verify that no function can be called after all owners are removed

original_hypothesis.preliminary_attack_scenario: A compromised owner key holder calls removeOwnerAtIndex for every active owner index (including their own). After the last removal, the wallet has zero owners, and all onlyOwner/onlyEntryPointOrOwner functions revert, permanently locking all ETH, tokens, and NFTs.
prerequisites: At least one owner key is compromised or malicious

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Permanent loss of all wallet funds
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All wallet co-owners and the wallet itself
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: None — no function can add new owners without owner authorization

original_hypothesis.test_coverage_gap: No test for zero-owner state reachability and fund recovery

technical_investigation_focus.gas_analysis_needed: Minimal — N removeOwnerAtIndex calls where N is owner count

validation_result.impact_summary: The last owner can remove themselves via removeOwnerAtIndex, leaving zero owners and permanently bricking the wallet with no recovery path for any locked funds.
validation_result.detailed_analysis: The removeOwnerAtIndex function in MultiOwnable.sol has no check to ensure at least one owner remains after removal. The onlyOwner modifier (via _checkOwner) validates the caller is an owner BEFORE the deletion occurs, so the last remaining owner can successfully remove themselves. After all owners are removed: (1) no address passes _checkOwner(), blocking all onlyOwner functions including addOwnerAddress, addOwnerPublicKey, and _authorizeUpgrade; (2) the EntryPoint path fails because _validateSignature will get empty bytes from ownerAtIndex() and revert with InvalidOwnerBytesLength; (3) initialize() cannot be re-called because nextOwnerIndex > 0 permanently (monotonically increasing). This creates an irrecoverable state where all funds are permanently locked.
validation_result.code_evidence_assessment: The removeOwnerAtIndex function at MultiOwnable.sol lines 97-105 clearly shows: (1) onlyOwner check happens in the modifier before function body executes, (2) only NoOwnerAtIndex revert exists for empty slots, (3) no minimum count check exists, (4) no activeOwnerCount variable exists anywhere. The _checkOwner function at lines 179-184 shows that after zero owners, no external caller can pass the check. The initialize function in CoinbaseSmartWallet.sol checks nextOwnerIndex() != 0, which remains true even with zero active owners since nextOwnerIndex only increments.

vulnerable_code_snippets.vulnerability_explanation: No check that at least one owner remains after removal. The onlyOwner modifier validates the caller before deletion, so the last owner can remove themselves, leaving zero owners and permanently bricking the wallet.
After all owners are removed, isOwnerAddress returns false for every address, and msg.sender != address(this) for external calls, making all onlyOwner functions permanently inaccessible.
Re-initialization is impossible even after all owners are removed because nextOwnerIndex is monotonically increasing and will be > 0, so the Initialized() revert always triggers.

calling_sequence: 1. Owner calls removeOwnerAtIndex(indexOfOtherOwner) - removes other owners first
2. Owner calls removeOwnerAtIndex(ownIndex) - removes themselves last
3. onlyOwner modifier calls _checkOwner() which passes because caller is still registered at this point
4. Function body executes: delete isOwner[owner] and delete ownerAtIndex[index]
5. After return, zero owners exist in the contract
6. All onlyOwner functions now permanently revert
7. validateUserOp fails because _validateSignature gets empty ownerBytes for any index
8. initialize() reverts with Initialized() because nextOwnerIndex > 0
execution_path_analysis.vulnerable_code_path: For a wallet with a single owner Alice at index 0: Alice calls removeOwnerAtIndex(0). The onlyOwner modifier at line 93 calls _checkOwner() (line 179-184). isOwnerAddress(Alice) returns true (line 110: _getMultiOwnableStorage().isOwner[abi.encode(Alice)] is true). The modifier passes. Function body at lines 98-104 executes: ownerAtIndex(0) returns Alice's bytes, length check passes, then delete isOwner[Alice] and delete ownerAtIndex[0]. Now zero owners exist. No recovery: addOwnerAddress requires onlyOwner (reverts), initialize checks nextOwnerIndex != 0 (reverts with Initialized), EntryPoint path requires valid owner signature (ownerAtIndex returns empty bytes, _validateSignature reverts with InvalidOwnerBytesLength at CoinbaseSmartWallet.sol line 308).

config_validation.deployment_evidence: No deployment scripts provided. The defect is in core contract logic independent of deployment configuration.

validation_result.attack_scenario_validation: Scenario: A wallet has 3 owners at indices 0, 1, 2. A compromised owner (index 0) calls removeOwnerAtIndex(1), then removeOwnerAtIndex(2), then removeOwnerAtIndex(0). Each call passes the onlyOwner check because the attacker is still registered. After the third call, zero owners remain. All funds (ETH, ERC-20, NFTs) are permanently locked. Alternatively, a single-owner wallet (non-malicious) could accidentally brick their wallet by calling removeOwnerAtIndex on their own index. The attack requires only knowledge of owner indices (publicly queryable via nextOwnerIndex and ownerAtIndex) and costs minimal gas (N removeOwnerAtIndex calls).
validation_result.impact_assessment: CRITICAL - Permanent, irrecoverable loss of all wallet funds. No on-chain recovery mechanism exists: initialize() is blocked by the Initialized guard, all owner management functions require onlyOwner which permanently fails, and the EntryPoint validation path fails because no valid owner exists for signature verification. Any ETH, ERC-20 tokens, or NFTs held by the wallet become permanently inaccessible.
recommended_mitigations: Add an active owner counter that is incremented on add and decremented on remove, with a require(ownerCount > 1) check in removeOwnerAtIndex
Alternatively, prevent an owner from removing themselves (require that ownerAtIndex(index) != abi.encode(msg.sender))
Consider adding a minimum owner count parameter configurable at initialization time
Add an emergency recovery mechanism (e.g., social recovery or time-locked guardian)
validation_result.replication_plan: 1. Deploy CoinbaseSmartWalletFactory with implementation. 2. Create wallet with single EOA owner via createAccount. 3. Send ETH to the wallet. 4. As the owner, call removeOwnerAtIndex(0). 5. Verify that addOwnerAddress reverts with Unauthorized. 6. Verify that execute reverts with Unauthorized. 7. Verify that initialize reverts with Initialized. 8. Verify ETH is permanently locked.
additional_testing_needed: Verify that the EntryPoint path also fails after all owners are removed (validateUserOp should fail at signature validation)
Test batch removal via executeBatch to confirm all owners can be removed in a single transaction
Verify no edge case in Solady UUPSUpgradeable allows recovery without owner authorization
